fund.name: BANK OF INDIA Flexi Cap Fund Direct Plan -Growth
fund.fund_house: Bank of India Investment Managers Private Limited

allocations.rationale: Your preferred Flexi Cap entry point. BoI Flexi Cap has delivered 19.5% CAGR over 5 years with impressive alpha of 18.3% — beating peers through active selection across the cap spectrum. At 1% fee, it's competitive. This fund gives you the flexible allocation you appreciate: it can swing between large and small caps based on valuations, rather than being locked into a rigid index formula. Since you read factsheets the way others read novels, you'll note its Sharpe ratio of 1.1 reflects excellent risk-adjusted returns. The small AUM (₹515 Cr) keeps it nimble without diluting performance.

fund.name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Large cap anchor for stability and liquidity. Nippon India Large Cap's 17.7% 5-year CAGR with 0.98 beta (nearly market-matched) offers consistent outperformance without excessive risk. Its tight tracking error (3.16%) and seasoned management (18 years old, ₹16.6k Cr AUM) give you a rock-solid core. At 22 years to retirement, you need this ballast — it will cushion downturns and compound steadily. The 1% fee is standard for actively managed large caps.

fund.name: Edelweiss Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap pick for long-term growth acceleration. Edelweiss Mid Cap delivered 21.7% CAGR over 5 years with alpha of 15.17% — evidence of genuine stock-picking skill. The 1.15% fee is standard for mid-caps and justified by the outperformance. Its Sharpe ratio of 1.15 is excellent (higher than many large-cap peers), meaning you get more return per unit of risk. At age 36, you have 22 years: mid-caps historically return ~13-15% over long periods, and this fund beats that mark significantly. A ₹18k/month SIP here seeds meaningful compounding in this higher-growth segment.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Passive mid-cap diversification. The Nifty Midcap 150 index at 0.2% fee offers a mathematically pure mid-cap play — no manager skill required, no alpha decay risk. It's returned 18.7% over 5 years, tracking the broader 150-company midcap universe. Use this for 15% (via SIP only) to balance the active Edelweiss Mid Cap pick above. This dual approach — active + passive in the same segment — gives you both a high-conviction active bet AND index-like diversification. The ultra-low 0.2% fee (40x cheaper than active mid-caps) also protects you against fee drag if the manager underperforms.

fund.name: Aditya Birla Sun Life Medium Term Plan - Growth - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Aditya Birla Sun Life AMC Limited

allocations.rationale: Debt allocation for downside cushion and emergency liquidity. Medium-term debt is slab-taxed (you'll pay 30% on gains), so a 9.6% pre-tax return becomes ~6.7% post-tax — but still beats FDs and guarantees capital stability. AB Medium Term has returned 12.7% over 5 years (post-tax: ~9%), with Sharpe ratio of 2.24 (exceptional risk-adjusted return). This 10% allocation gives you ₹9k/month passive income, plus a steady ₹250k lumpsum deployment. With ₹2.5Cr existing corpus, this helps you build the ₹1Cr emergency buffer (goal) without over-exposure to equity volatility in your safety bucket.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Account Setup (2 hours) — Open a direct mutual fund account on Kuvera, Groww, or MFU. Link your bank account for SIPs. This is tax-efficient vs. regular (commissioned) plans.

Step 2: Lumpsum Deployment (Week 1) — You have ₹2.5Cr existing corpus. From this, invest ₹2.125Cr across the five funds as per the lumpsum amounts above. Leave ₹375Cr aside in a liquid overnight fund for your emergency buffer — this earns ~5.5% and is entirely tax-efficient.

Step 3: Start Monthly SIPs (Immediate) — Set up five SIP mandates for ₹90k/month total: ₹27k to BoI Flexi Cap, ₹22.5k to Nippon Large Cap, ₹18k to Edelweiss Mid Cap, ₹13.5k to Motilal Oswal Midcap 150 Index, ₹9k to AB Medium Term Debt. Configure SIPs for the 5th of each month. All direct plans use growth option.

Step 4: Annual SIP Step-Up (Year 2 onwards) — Increase each SIP by 10% every January. This mimics your career progression and keeps pace with inflation.

Step 5: First Month Checklist — Confirm all five SIPs are active. Download factsheets for each fund. Check your portfolio via the app; it should show ₹2.125Cr in equities/debt and ₹375Cr in liquid. Note: NAV updates after market close (~4:30 PM IST).
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Confirm all five SIPs have gone through (₹270k total deployed). Check if any fund has not credited units.
After 6 months: Review each fund's 6-month performance. If any fund underperforms peers by >3%, flag for analysis — but don't panic-switch; one quarter is noise.
After 12 months: Run full-year performance check. Portfolio should have ≥12% CAGR (base case). Review 3-year track records. Increase all SIPs by 10% as planned.
After 3 years (age 39): Check if equity allocation has drifted >5% from 80%. If so, shift 5-10% to debt to rebalance. Expected portfolio value: ~₹5.5-6.5Cr.

Annually (every Dec): Print year-end statement and file with tax records. Email factsheets to CA 30 days before ITR filing. If any fund chronically underperforms (Sharpe ratio dropped >0.2), consider a switch.
Red flags to act immediately: Fund closes; fund manager leaves and replacement is weaker; fund AUM falls below ₹50Cr; major life change (job loss, large liability, relocation).
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Target allocation: 80% Equity + 10% Debt. Rebalance once a year (December) or when any category drifts >5% from target. If equity booms to 85%, sell ₹150L from your highest-performing equity fund and buy ₹150L of debt. Sell debt first (already slab-taxed). Sell equities only after holding >1 year (you'll qualify for 12.5% LTCG on gains >₹1.25L). If you get a windfall (₹50L bonus), invest 80% in equity (split equally among your three active equity picks) and 20% in debt. Do not overweight the best-performing fund of the moment.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
This plan is **predominantly active with disciplined passive hedging**. 

**Passive Instrument Fraction (0.167):** Of the 90% equity allocation, only 15% flows to the Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund (passive). The remaining 75% (BoI Flexi Cap 30%, Nippon Large Cap 25%, Edelweiss Mid Cap 20%) are actively managed funds. Debt is excluded per instructions.

**Turnover Score (0.85):** The plan emphasizes buy-and-hold discipline with annual rebalancing (December only, triggered only if drift exceeds 5%). Monthly SIPs are systematic, not tactical. Review checkpoints are annual, not quarterly. The rationale explicitly states "don't panic-switch; one quarter is noise." This reflects very low portfolio turnover — highly passive in execution cadence.

**Cost Emphasis Score (0.35):** While cost is acknowledged ("ultra-low 0.2% fee" for the index fund, "1% is competitive/standard"), it is NOT the primary selection criterion. Fund choices are driven by alpha and outperformance: BoI's 18.3% alpha and "active selection across the cap spectrum," Edelweiss's "genuine stock-picking skill," and Nippon's "consistent outperformance." Cost serves as secondary justification, not primary decision driver.

**Research vs. Cost Score (0.40):** The plan shows moderate research emphasis on fund-level analysis (manager track records, Sharpe ratios, alpha metrics, tenure, AUM). However, there is no deep security-level research (no company analysis, PEG ratios, moat evaluation). The inclusion of a passive index fund and the acknowledgment that it protects against "alpha decay risk" and manager underperformance reflect a pragmatic, diversified approach rather than pure conviction-driven research.

**Time Horizon Alignment Score (0.90):** The 22-year horizon to retirement (age 36 to 58) is explicit throughout. Strategy relies on systematic annual SIP step-ups (10% yearly), long-term compounding, and minimal intervention. The plan dismisses short-term volatility and emphasizes patience. No tactical windows, event-driven moves, or market-timing language appears.

**Portfolio Activeness Score (0.38):** Weighted activeness across all positions: BoI Flexi (27% of portfolio, beta 0.87, alpha +18.3 = genuinely active); Edelweiss Mid (18% of portfolio, alpha +15.17 = genuinely active); Nippon Large (22.5% of portfolio, beta 0.98, alpha +4.27, tight 3.16% tracking error = closet indexer, mostly passive); Motilal Index (13.5% of portfolio, pure index fund, beta 1.03 = passive); AB Debt (10% of portfolio, active bond management). Portfolio-weighted: ~55% clearly active alpha-seeking, ~13.5% passive index, ~22.5% borderline/closet indexing, ~10% active debt. This yields a portfolio that is moderately active overall, with genuine stock-picking conviction in 2–3 core positions offset by one passive index hedge.

pqs.reasoning:
STRENGTHS: The plan delivers a sensible 80-20 equity-debt split appropriate for moderate risk and 22-year horizon, with detailed fund rationales that reflect the investor's preference for nuanced analysis. Fund house diversification is good (5 fund houses), active-passive mix is balanced, and the 12.6% base return projection is realistic. Rebalancing guidelines and review checkpoints are well-structured.

CRITICAL GAPS:

1. GOAL ALIGNMENT (0.65): Plan lacks explicit mapping to stated goals. Children's education (12 & 15 years) has no dedicated allocation or time-based segmentation. Retirement target of ₹8Cr at 55 is not explicitly projected; investor's timeline is 19 years (age 36→55) but plan references 22 years inconsistently. International diversification is a stated preference but entirely absent (no global equity, no international fund). Emergency buffer (₹1Cr goal) is mentioned in setup (₹375Cr, likely typo for ₹37.5L) but omitted from the allocations table, creating ambiguity about what funds the 10th % of corpus.

2. INTERNAL CONSISTENCY (0.48): Multiple structural contradictions:
   - Timeline: Profile states 22-year horizon; investor is 36 and wants to retire at 55 = 19 years. Plan says "22 years to retirement" in rationale but this conflicts with the stated goal.
   - Allocation math: Table shows 30+25+20+15+10 = 100%, but stated target is "80% Equity + 10% Debt" = 90%. The unaccounted 10% (emergency buffer in liquid overnight fund) is mentioned in setup but NOT in allocations table or percentage breakdown.
   - ELSS omission: No explanation for why ELSS (critical for 30% slab investor with ₹1.5L annual 80C headroom) is completely absent.
   - International: Stated preference ignored without rationale.
   - Risks array is empty (no explicit risk warnings).
   - Debt selection lacks tax justification.

3. TAX EFFICIENCY (0.30): This is the plan's most critical failure for a 30%-slab investor.
   - **ELSS missing**: With ₹10.8L annual investible surplus, a 30%-slab investor can claim ₹1.5L/year under 80C. Allocating ₹1.5L to ELSS yields ₹45k annual tax savings PLUS equity taxation (12.5% LTCG). This is foregone entirely.
   - **Suboptimal debt choice**: Medium-Term Debt is slab-taxed (30% on gains). For identical risk, an Aggressive Hybrid (≥65% equity) would be taxed at 12.5% LTCG — a 17.5% tax advantage. Plan mentions "9.6% pre-tax → 6.7% post-tax" but never asks whether Aggressive Hybrid would be better. This is a ₹17.5k+ annual optimization miss (on ₹250k debt allocation).
   - **No tax rationale**: While the debt fund's tax treatment is acknowledged, there is NO explanation of why equity funds are chosen (their 12.5% LTCG benefit) or why slab-taxed alternatives were rejected. For a "reads factsheets like novels" investor in the highest slab, this omission is striking.

4. DIVERSIFICATION (0.62): Good breadth but notable gaps:
   - Fund house diversity: Excellent (BoI, Nippon, Edelweiss, Motilal Oswal, AB).
   - Active/passive mix: Good (3 active equity, 1 passive midcap index).
   - But: Only 1 debt fund (concentration risk). No small-cap exposure. No multi-asset, arbitrage, or international funds. No ELSS (which would add both tax benefit and cap-spectrum diversification). Geographic concentration: 100% India (stated international preference unmet).

5. RISK-RETURN APPROPRIATENESS (0.70): Reasonable overall but incomplete.
   - 80% equity for moderate risk, 22-year horizon is sound.
   - Base case 12.6% CAGR is realistic for the fund mix.
   - Bear case 8.6% may be optimistic (no -40% or recession scenario explored).
   - Rebalancing rules and drift triggers are sensible.
   - But no stress testing, no scenario for early retirement need, job loss, or major liability shock. Downside analysis is light.

RECOMMENDATIONS TO IMPROVE: (1) Reframe timeline as 19 years (retire at 55 from age 36), not 22. (2) Add ELSS with ₹1.5L annual allocation (moves ₹1.5L from BoI Flexi Cap to ELSS SIP). (3) Replace Medium-Term Debt (₹250k, ₹9k SIP) with Aggressive Hybrid or indexed arbitrage (same risk, 12.5% tax vs. 30%). (4) Define emergency buffer properly in allocations (e.g., 10% in overnight liquid fund, explicitly listed). (5) Justify international omission or add a global fund allocation. (6) Map education goal to a time-bucketed sub-allocation. (7) Populate risks array with manager concentration, slab-tax risk, and market/volatility scenarios. (8) Add explicit tax rationale section citing 80C and LTCG benefits. For a 30%-slab investor with this investor's sophistication, the plan's tax blindness is a significant weakness.
